ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Taíno were the first indigenous peoples to encounter Columbus in 1492. Spanish colonization caused catastrophic demographic collapse through enslavement under the encomienda system, massacres, forced labor in gold mines, and introduction of European diseases. Pre-contact population estimates for Hispaniola range from 250,000 to 3 million; by 1548 fewer than 500 Taíno survived on the island. This constitutes one of the earliest and most complete genocides of the colonial era. ETHICS-001: The endonym 'Taíno' may not have been a unified self-designation; it was recorded by Spanish chroniclers as meaning 'good' or 'noble.' The five cacicazgos of Hispaniola (Magua, Maguaná, Marién, Higüey, Xaragua) were politically distinct. Modern genetic studies have documented surviving Taíno ancestry in Caribbean populations, challenging the narrative of total extinction.

name_variants.name: Taíno
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial term derived from the word the indigenous people reportedly used to distinguish themselves from Island Caribs
name_variants.source: Rouse, Irving. The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus (1992)

name_variants.name: Taíno Confederation
name_variants.context: English academic convention; 'confederation' is an approximation of the cacicazgo political system
name_variants.source: Wilson, Samuel M. Hispaniola: Caribbean Chiefdoms in the Age of Columbus (1990)

name_variants.name: Lucayans
name_variants.context: Name for Taíno subgroup inhabiting the Bahamas; from Lukku-Cairi ('island people')
name_variants.source: Keegan, William F. The People Who Discovered Columbus (1992)

territory_changes.description: Consolidation of Taíno chiefdom system across Hispaniola, Puerto Rico, Jamaica, Cuba, and the Bahamas. Development of complex cacicazgo polities with hierarchical social organization, ball courts, and monumental plazas. Agricultural intensification through conuco mound cultivation.

Columbus arrived on Hispaniola and established La Navidad. Initiated Spanish colonization that would destroy Taíno civilization. Cacique Guacanagaríx initially cooperated but resistance grew as Spanish demands for gold and labor intensified.

Near-total demographic collapse of the Taíno population through encomienda forced labor, massacres (notably the Higüey massacre of 1503 and Xaragua massacre of 1503 under Ovando), and epidemic disease. Bartolomé de las Casas documented the systematic destruction. Population dropped from hundreds of thousands to fewer than 500 on Hispaniola.

sources.citation: Rouse, Irving. The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus. Yale University Press, 1992
Wilson, Samuel M. Hispaniola: Caribbean Chiefdoms in the Age of Columbus. University of Alabama Press, 1990
Las Casas, Bartolomé de. Brevísima relación de la destrucción de las Indias, 1552 — primary eyewitness account of Spanish atrocities against Taíno

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Jamaican Maroons were enslaved Africans and their descendants who escaped slavery and formed autonomous communities in Jamaica's mountainous interior. Their history is inseparable from the transatlantic slave trade and the institution of chattel slavery. The First Maroon War (1728-1739) was one of the most successful resistance campaigns by enslaved peoples in the Americas. Queen Nanny, an Ashanti-descended leader, is now a Jamaican National Hero. The 1739 treaties granted Maroon autonomy but controversially required them to return future runaway enslaved people — a moral compromise forced by colonial power asymmetry. ETHICS-001: 'Maroon' likely derives from Spanish 'cimarrón' (wild/untamed), a colonial term; the Maroons' own self-designations varied by community.

name_variants.name: Windward Maroons
name_variants.context: Eastern Jamaica Maroon communities in the Blue Mountains, led by Queen Nanny
name_variants.source: Campbell, Mavis C. The Maroons of Jamaica 1655-1796 (1988)

name_variants.name: Leeward Maroons / Cudjoe's Town
name_variants.context: Western Jamaica Maroon communities in Cockpit Country, led by Captain Cudjoe
name_variants.source: Bilby, Kenneth M. True-Born Maroons (2005)

name_variants.name: Cimarrones de Jamaica
name_variants.context: Spanish-origin term; the first Maroons escaped from Spanish-owned plantations when Britain captured Jamaica in 1655
name_variants.source: Price, Richard (ed.). Maroon Societies: Rebel Slave Communities in the Americas (1996)

territory_changes.region: Blue Mountains and Cockpit Country, Jamaica
territory_changes.description: When the English captured Jamaica from Spain, enslaved Africans held by the Spanish fled to the mountainous interior and established autonomous communities. They merged with pre-existing communities of runaways from the Spanish period. The inaccessible mountain terrain provided natural fortification.

First Maroon War began. British colonial forces launched sustained military campaigns against Maroon settlements. Queen Nanny led guerrilla resistance in the Blue Mountains; Captain Cudjoe led resistance in the Cockpit Country. The Maroons used terrain advantage and guerrilla tactics to fight the much larger British forces to a stalemate.

Treaty of 1739 between Cudjoe and the British recognized Maroon autonomy over 1,500 acres. A separate treaty was signed with the Windward Maroons. The treaties granted self-governance but required Maroons to return future runaway enslaved people and assist the British in suppressing slave rebellions — terms imposed under duress.

sources.citation: Campbell, Mavis C. The Maroons of Jamaica 1655-1796: A History of Resistance, Collaboration and Betrayal. Bergin & Garvey, 1988
Bilby, Kenneth M. True-Born Maroons. University Press of Florida, 2005
Price, Richard (ed.). Maroon Societies: Rebel Slave Communities in the Americas. Johns Hopkins University Press, 3rd ed., 1996

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Apache peoples endured centuries of colonial violence from Spain, Mexico, and the United States. Spanish slave raids beginning in the 1600s targeted Apache communities. The U.S. military waged decades of warfare (1849-1886) culminating in the forced surrender of Geronimo and the Chiricahua Apache. The entire Chiricahua band — including scouts who had served the U.S. Army — were deported as prisoners of war to Florida and Alabama (1886-1913), suffering catastrophic mortality. Children were forcibly removed to boarding schools (Carlisle Indian Industrial School and others) to destroy Apache language and culture. ETHICS-001: 'Apache' is likely a Zuni word meaning 'enemy'; the peoples' own endonyms include Ndé, Inde, Nné, and Dine'é depending on band. Using the colonial exonym as primary is a concession to searchability.

name_variants.name: Apache
name_variants.context: Colonial exonym, likely from Zuni 'apachu' (enemy) or Yavapai 'epache' (people). Became the standard English term
name_variants.source: Opler, Morris E. An Apache Life-Way (1941)

name_variants.name: Ndé
name_variants.context: Western Apache / Chiricahua endonym meaning 'the people'
name_variants.source: Basso, Keith H. Wisdom Sits in Places (1996)

name_variants.name: N'dee / Inde
name_variants.context: Mescalero Apache endonym; variant dialectal forms across Apache bands
name_variants.source: Opler, Morris E. Myths and Tales of the Mescalero Apache Indians (1938)

territory_changes.description: Athabaskan-speaking peoples migrated southward from subarctic Canada and established territory across present-day Arizona, New Mexico, western Texas, and northern Mexico (Sonora, Chihuahua). Diversified into distinct bands: Chiricahua, Mescalero, Jicarilla, Western Apache, Lipan, and Kiowa-Apache.

territory_changes.region: Apache territory across new U.S.-Mexico border
territory_changes.description: Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo placed most Apache territory under U.S. sovereignty. The U.S. launched sustained military campaigns. Apache leaders including Mangas Coloradas, Cochise, Victorio, and Geronimo led armed resistance across the Southwest. Mangas Coloradas was murdered under a flag of truce in 1863.

Geronimo's surrender at Skeleton Canyon ended the last major Apache military resistance. The entire Chiricahua band (approximately 500 men, women, and children, including loyal U.S. Army scouts) were deported as prisoners of war to Fort Marion, Florida, then Mount Vernon Barracks, Alabama. High mortality from disease in humid lowland conditions. Not released until 1913 — 27 years as POWs. Surviving Apache peoples were confined to reservations: San Carlos, Fort Apache, Mescalero.

sources.citation: Opler, Morris E. An Apache Life-Way: The Economic, Social, and Religious Institutions of the Chiricahua Indians. University of Chicago Press, 1941
Sweeney, Edwin R. From Cochise to Geronimo: The Chiricahua Apaches, 1874-1886. University of Oklahoma Press, 2010
Stockel, H. Henrietta. Survival of the Spirit: Chiricahua Apaches in Captivity. University of Nevada Press, 1993

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Navajo (Diné) endured the Long Walk of 1864 — a forced deportation of approximately 8,000 to 10,000 Diné people marched 300+ miles to the Bosque Redondo concentration camp at Fort Sumner, New Mexico, under orders of Kit Carson and General James Carleton. Hundreds died during the march and at the camp from starvation, disease, and exposure. The Diné homeland was systematically destroyed: orchards cut, hogans burned, livestock slaughtered. The Treaty of 1868 allowed return to a fraction of traditional territory but the trauma remains central to Diné identity. Forced boarding school attendance (1880s-1960s) attempted cultural destruction. ETHICS-001: 'Navajo' is a Spanish colonial exonym from Tewa 'Navahu'u'; the people call themselves Diné ('the people') and their land Diné Bikéyah.

name_variants.name: Navajo Nation
name_variants.context: English name used since the Treaty of 1868 established the reservation; 'Navajo' from Spanish via Tewa
name_variants.source: Iverson, Peter. Diné: A History of the Navajos. University of New Mexico Press, 2002

name_variants.name: Diné
name_variants.context: Endonym meaning 'the people'; the Navajo Nation officially uses both Diné and Navajo
name_variants.source: Iverson, Peter & Roessel, Monty. Diné: A History of the Navajos (2002)

name_variants.name: Provincia de Navajó
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial designation; earliest recorded use by Fray Gerónimo de Zárate Salmerón in 1626
name_variants.source: Brugge, David M. Navajos in the Catholic Church Records of New Mexico, 1694-1875 (1968)

territory_changes.region: Four Corners region (present-day AZ, NM, UT, CO)
territory_changes.description: Diné peoples established extensive pastoral and agricultural territory across the Four Corners region after migrating from the north. Adopted sheep herding from Puebloan and Spanish contact, developing a distinctive agropastoral way of life centered on the four sacred mountains.

The Long Walk: U.S. Army under Kit Carson destroyed Diné food sources, orchards, and livestock, then forced approximately 8,000-10,000 Diné to march 300+ miles to the Bosque Redondo internment camp at Fort Sumner. Hundreds died en route and at the camp. Constitutes ethnic cleansing and forced displacement.

territory_changes.region: Reduced Diné Bikéyah (northeastern Arizona, northwestern New Mexico)
territory_changes.description: Treaty of Bosque Redondo allowed the Diné to return to a reservation comprising a fraction of their original territory. The treaty established a 3.5-million-acre reservation. Subsequent executive orders expanded it, and the modern Navajo Nation covers approximately 27,000 square miles — the largest reservation in the U.S.

sources.citation: Iverson, Peter & Roessel, Monty. Diné: A History of the Navajos. University of New Mexico Press, 2002
Bailey, Lynn R. The Long Walk: A History of the Navajo Wars, 1846-68. Westernlore Press, 1964
Treaty between the United States and the Navajo Tribe of Indians, June 1, 1868 (15 Stat. 667)

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Seminole Nation formed from diverse indigenous peoples and escaped enslaved Africans (Black Seminoles) in Spanish Florida. The U.S. fought three Seminole Wars (1817-58) — the most costly Indian wars in American history — to seize Florida and forcibly remove the Seminole. The Indian Removal Act of 1830 mandated deportation to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Approximately 3,000 Seminole were forcibly marched westward; hundreds died. A remnant of perhaps 200-300 resisted removal and retreated into the Everglades, never signing a peace treaty — making the Seminole one of the only Native nations never to formally surrender to the U.S. The Black Seminoles, who had been allies and community members, were particularly targeted; some were re-enslaved after capture. ETHICS-001: 'Seminole' likely from Creek 'simanó-li' (wild, separatist); a term applied externally.

name_variants.name: Seminole
name_variants.context: English form from Creek/Muskogee 'simanó-li'; became standard designation
name_variants.source: Covington, James W. The Seminoles of Florida. University Press of Florida, 1993

name_variants.name: Isti Semoli
name_variants.context: Muskogee/Creek term meaning 'wild people' or 'separatists', referring to those who moved south into Florida
name_variants.source: Weisman, Brent R. Unconquered People (1999)

name_variants.name: yat'siminoli
name_variants.context: Alternate Muskogee form; the Seminole were a multiethnic confederation including Muskogee, Hitchiti, Mikasuki speakers, and Black Seminoles
name_variants.source: Sturtevant, William C. Creek into Seminole, in North American Indians in Historical Perspective (1971)

territory_changes.description: Seminole ethnogenesis: diverse Creek, Hitchiti, Yamassee, and other southeastern peoples migrated into Florida following the destruction of earlier Florida indigenous populations by English-allied raids. They established autonomous communities in the power vacuum left by the collapse of Spanish mission populations.

Indian Removal Act signed. Three Seminole Wars (1817-18, 1835-42, 1855-58) fought to forcibly remove the Seminole. The Second Seminole War was the longest and costliest Indian war in U.S. history. Osceola, the most prominent leader, was captured under a flag of truce. Approximately 3,000 Seminole were forcibly relocated to Oklahoma.

Third Seminole War ended inconclusively. A remnant of 200-300 Seminole retreated deep into the Everglades and never surrendered. They are ancestors of today's Seminole Tribe of Florida. The Seminole in Oklahoma organized separately. No formal peace treaty was ever signed — the Seminole are known as the 'Unconquered People.'

sources.citation: Covington, James W. The Seminoles of Florida. University Press of Florida, 1993
Weisman, Brent R. Unconquered People: Florida's Seminole and Miccosukee Indians. University Press of Florida, 1999
Missall, John & Missall, Mary Lou. The Seminole Wars: America's Longest Indian Conflict. University Press of Florida, 2004

name_original: Nation métisse / La Nâsyon di Michif
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Métis are a distinct Indigenous people of mixed European (primarily French-Canadian) and First Nations (primarily Cree, Ojibwe, Saulteaux) ancestry who developed their own language (Michif), culture, and political identity on the Red River. The Canadian government's imposition of colonial land survey systems over Métis river-lot holdings precipitated the Red River Resistance (1869-70) and the North-West Resistance (1885). Louis Riel, the Métis leader, was executed by the Canadian government in 1885 in what many consider a political murder. The Métis were dispossessed of their lands through the scrip system — nominally land grants that were manipulated and defrauded by speculators. Métis land rights remain contested. ETHICS-001: The Métis developed Michif, a unique creole language blending French and Cree, which is now critically endangered.

name_variants.name: Métis Nation
name_variants.context: French-origin name; 'métis' from Latin 'mixtus' (mixed). Standard designation for the nation
name_variants.source: Ens, Gerhard & Sawchuk, Joe. From New Peoples to New Nations (2016)

name_variants.name: Red River Métis
name_variants.context: English designation specifying the Red River community, the political heart of the Métis nation
name_variants.source: Payment, Diane. The Free People — Otipemisiwak (2009)

name_variants.name: Bois-Brûlés / Chicots
name_variants.context: Colloquial French terms; 'Bois-Brûlés' (burnt wood) was a self-designation referring to skin color
name_variants.source: Shore, Fred. The Emergence of the Métis Nation in Manitoba, in Barkwell et al. (2001)

territory_changes.description: Battle of Seven Oaks: Métis under Cuthbert Grant defeated Hudson's Bay Company forces, asserting Métis territorial sovereignty over the Red River. This event is considered a founding moment of Métis national consciousness. Grant was subsequently recognized as 'Warden of the Plains.'

Red River Resistance: Louis Riel and the Métis established a provisional government to negotiate terms of entry into Canadian Confederation, resisting the transfer of Rupert's Land from the HBC to Canada without Métis consent. Resulted in the Manitoba Act (1870) creating the province of Manitoba with nominal Métis land protections.

North-West Resistance: Métis under Riel and Gabriel Dumont took up arms at Batoche after years of ignored petitions for land rights. Defeated by Canadian militia. Riel was tried and executed for treason on November 16, 1885. The defeat ended Métis political autonomy and accelerated dispossession through the fraudulent scrip system.

sources.citation: Ens, Gerhard & Sawchuk, Joe. From New Peoples to New Nations: Aspects of Métis History and Identity from the Eighteenth to Twenty-first Centuries. University of Toronto Press, 2016
Payment, Diane. The Free People — Otipemisiwak: Batoche, Saskatchewan, 1870-1930. University of Ottawa Press, 2009
Sprague, D.N. Canada and the Métis, 1869-1885. Wilfrid Laurier University Press, 1988

capital_name: No single settlement (nomadic bands across Isla Grande)
ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: GENOCIDE. The Selk'nam people of Tierra del Fuego were systematically exterminated by European settlers, primarily sheep ranchers, in the late 19th and early 20th centuries. Ranchers organized hunting parties that paid bounties for Selk'nam ears, heads, or entire bodies. The Sociedad Explotadora de Tierra del Fuego was directly complicit. Salesian missionaries, while opposing the killings, confined survivors to missions where European diseases caused further mass death. Julius Popper and others openly boasted of killing Selk'nam. From an estimated 3,000-4,000 people in 1880, the population fell to approximately 500 by 1905 and the last fully Selk'nam person, Ángela Loij, died in 1974. This genocide has been largely absent from official Chilean and Argentine national narratives. ETHICS-001: 'Ona' is an exonym from the Yaghan language; 'Selk'nam' is the people's own name.

name_variants.name: Selk'nam
name_variants.context: Endonym; the people's own name for themselves
name_variants.source: Chapman, Anne. European Encounters with the Yamana People of Cape Horn, Before and After Darwin (2010)

name_variants.name: Ona
name_variants.context: Exonym from the Yaghan language; widely used in earlier literature but increasingly replaced by the endonym Selk'nam
name_variants.source: Gusinde, Martin. Die Feuerland-Indianer, Vol. 1: Die Selk'nam (1931)

name_variants.name: Onas / Pueblo Ona
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial/settler term used during and after the genocide; based on the Yaghan exonym
name_variants.source: Borrero, Luis Alberto. Los Selk'nam (Onas) (2001)

territory_changes.region: Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego
territory_changes.description: Archaeological evidence of human habitation on Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego dating to approximately 10,000 years ago. The Selk'nam occupied the northern and central plains of the island, subsisting primarily on guanaco hunting. They maintained a complex cosmological and ceremonial system including the Hain initiation ceremony.

territory_changes.region: Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego
territory_changes.description: Sheep ranching operations, particularly by the Sociedad Explotadora de Tierra del Fuego, began appropriating Selk'nam hunting lands. As guanaco were displaced by sheep, Selk'nam hunted sheep for survival, provoking rancher retaliation. A systematic campaign of extermination began with bounty hunting.

territory_changes.region: Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego
territory_changes.description: Systematic extermination of the Selk'nam was at its peak. Ranchers paid bounties for body parts. Julius Popper organized armed expeditions. Survivors were confined to Salesian missions at Dawson Island and Río Grande where disease killed many more. From approximately 3,000-4,000 in 1880, the population fell to under 500 by 1905. The last full-blooded Selk'nam, Ángela Loij, died in 1974. Chile and Argentina have been slow to officially acknowledge this genocide.

sources.citation: Gusinde, Martin. Die Feuerland-Indianer, Band 1: Die Selk'nam. Mödling bei Wien, 1931 — the most comprehensive ethnographic record
Chapman, Anne. European Encounters with the Yamana People of Cape Horn, Before and After Darwin. Cambridge University Press, 2010
Borrero, Luis Alberto. Los Selk'nam (Onas): Evolución Cultural en la Isla Grande de Tierra del Fuego. Galerna, 2001

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Republic of Yucatan existed during a period of intense exploitation of the Maya majority by the Yucatecan creole elite. The Caste War (1847) erupted during this period as Maya people revolted against centuries of dispossession and forced labor. The Yucatecan government attempted to sell Maya captives as enslaved laborers to Cuba to fund the war. The republic's independence from Mexico was partly motivated by the creole elite's desire to maintain local control over Maya labor without Mexican federal interference. ETHICS-001: Yucatan's Maya-speaking majority had no political representation in the republic's government.

name_variants.name: Republic of Yucatan
name_variants.context: English translation of the official name
name_variants.source: Reed, Nelson. The Caste War of Yucatan (2001)

name_variants.name: República de Yucatán
name_variants.context: Official name of the breakaway state
name_variants.source: Rugeley, Terry. Yucatán's Maya Peasantry and the Origins of the Caste War (1996)

territory_changes.description: Yucatan declared independence from Mexico under Santiago Méndez, citing centralist policies from Mexico City that violated the 1824 federalist constitution. The peninsula had long-standing separatist sentiments and a distinct creole identity.

The Caste War began as Maya communities rose in armed revolt against centuries of land theft and forced labor by the creole elite. Maya forces nearly captured Mérida. The Yucatecan government appealed to Mexico, the U.S., Britain, and Spain for military help, offering sovereignty in exchange for intervention.

Yucatan rejoined Mexico in exchange for military assistance against the Maya insurgency. The Caste War would continue until 1901, with an autonomous Maya state (Chan Santa Cruz) persisting in eastern Yucatan.

sources.citation: Reed, Nelson. The Caste War of Yucatan. Stanford University Press, revised ed., 2001
Rugeley, Terry. Yucatán's Maya Peasantry and the Origins of the Caste War. University of Texas Press, 1996

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Republic of Texas was founded on land violently taken from Mexico and, more fundamentally, from indigenous peoples including the Comanche, Lipan Apache, Tonkawa, Karankawa, and Wichita. Anglo-American settlers, many from slaveholding states, brought chattel slavery with them; the protection of slavery was a central motivation for secession from Mexico, which had abolished slavery in 1829. The Texas constitution explicitly legalized slavery and barred free Black people from residency. Texas Rangers conducted campaigns of ethnic cleansing against Native nations and terrorized Mexican communities. The Comanche, who controlled most of western and central Texas, were not consulted in the creation of the republic. The Council House Fight (1840) — where Texas officials murdered Comanche peace negotiators — exemplifies the state's systematic bad faith. ETHICS-001: 'Texas' from Caddo 'taysha' (friend/ally); an ironic etymology given the republic's treatment of indigenous peoples.

name_variants.name: Republic of Texas
name_variants.context: Official English name
name_variants.source: Campbell, Randolph B. An Empire for Slavery: The Peculiar Institution in Texas (1989)

name_variants.name: República de Texas
name_variants.context: Spanish-language name; Mexico never recognized Texas independence
name_variants.source: Torget, Andrew J. Seeds of Empire: Cotton, Slavery, and the Transformation of the Texas Borderlands (2015)

name_variants.name: Lone Star Republic
name_variants.context: Popular nickname referencing the single-star flag
name_variants.source: Lack, Paul D. The Texas Revolutionary Experience (1992)

territory_changes.region: Texas (claimed territory from Rio Grande to 42nd parallel)
territory_changes.description: Anglo-American and Tejano settlers declared independence from Mexico after the battles of the Alamo and Goliad. Victory at San Jacinto (April 21, 1836) captured Santa Anna and effectively secured independence. Slavery was a central motivating factor, as Mexico had abolished it.

Council House Fight in San Antonio: Texas officials murdered 35 Comanche peace negotiators who came to discuss captive exchanges. Comanche retaliated with the Great Raid of 1840, striking deep into Anglo settlements. Campaigns of ethnic cleansing against indigenous peoples escalated throughout the republic period.
Texas was annexed by the United States on December 29, 1845, becoming the 28th state. Annexation was driven by pro-slavery expansion politics and provoked the Mexican-American War (1846-48).

sources.citation: Campbell, Randolph B. An Empire for Slavery: The Peculiar Institution in Texas, 1821-1865. Louisiana State University Press, 1989
Torget, Andrew J. Seeds of Empire: Cotton, Slavery, and the Transformation of the Texas Borderlands, 1800-1850. University of North Carolina Press, 2015
Hämäläinen, Pekka. The Comanche Empire. Yale University Press, 2008

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Second Mexican Empire was a European imperialist imposition. Napoleon III installed Austrian Archduke Maximilian as emperor of Mexico, using the pretext of debt collection to pursue French imperial ambitions in the Americas. The intervention violated Mexican sovereignty and was opposed by the legitimate republican government of Benito Juárez — a Zapotec indigenous leader. Mexican conservative elites and the Catholic Church collaborated with the French occupation. The French Foreign Legion and French expeditionary forces committed atrocities during the occupation. Juárez's eventual triumph represents both national sovereignty and indigenous resistance to European imperialism. ETHICS-001: Maximilian, paradoxically, held more liberal views than his Mexican conservative allies, supporting indigenous rights in theory while occupying the country by force.

name_variants.name: Second Mexican Empire
name_variants.context: English historiographic designation
name_variants.source: Hanna, Alfred J. & Hanna, Kathryn A. Napoleon III and Mexico (1971)

name_variants.name: Imperio de Maximiliano
name_variants.context: Colloquial Spanish designation identifying the empire with its imposed ruler
name_variants.source: Pani, Érika. Para mexicanizar el Segundo Imperio (2001)

territory_changes.description: French forces occupied Mexico City in June 1863. Maximilian of Habsburg arrived in 1864 and was proclaimed Emperor. Effective control was limited to central Mexico and areas garrisoned by French troops. Juárez's republican government continued to operate from northern Mexico, maintaining the legitimate government-in-exile.

Napoleon III, facing Prussian threats in Europe and U.S. pressure (invoking the Monroe Doctrine), began withdrawing French troops. Republican forces under Porfirio Díaz, Mariano Escobedo, and others rapidly recaptured territory. The empire collapsed militarily.

Maximilian was captured at Querétaro, court-martialed, and executed by firing squad on June 19, 1867, alongside generals Miramón and Mejía. Juárez's republic was fully restored. The defeat of the European-imposed empire is celebrated as a triumph of Mexican sovereignty.

sources.citation: Hanna, Alfred J. & Hanna, Kathryn A. Napoleon III and Mexico: American Triumph over Monarchy. University of North Carolina Press, 1971
Pani, Érika. Para mexicanizar el Segundo Imperio: El imaginario político de los imperialistas. El Colegio de México, 2001
Cunningham, Michele. Mexico and the Foreign Policy of Napoleon III. Palgrave Macmillan, 2001

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Empire of Brazil was the last country in the Western Hemisphere to abolish slavery (Lei Áurea, 1888). Brazil imported more enslaved Africans than any other country in the Americas — approximately 4.9 million people over the entire period of the slave trade. Under the empire, approximately 1.5 million enslaved people remained in bondage. The empire's economy was fundamentally built on enslaved labor in sugar, coffee, and mining. The Lei do Ventre Livre (1871) and Lei dos Sexagenários (1885) were incremental measures criticized as inadequate. Indigenous peoples in the Amazon, Cerrado, and Mata Atlântica continued to be dispossessed, murdered, and enslaved despite nominal protections. The Paraguayan War (1864-1870), in which the empire was a principal belligerent, devastated Paraguay's population. ETHICS-001: The empire's Portuguese-language institutions systematically marginalized indigenous and African-descendant populations.

name_variants.name: Empire of Brazil
name_variants.context: English translation
name_variants.source: Barman, Roderick J. Citizen Emperor: Pedro II and the Making of Brazil (1999)

name_variants.name: Império do Brasil
name_variants.context: Official Portuguese name
name_variants.source: Schwarcz, Lilia Moritz. The Emperor's Beard: Dom Pedro II and the Tropical Monarchy of Brazil (2004)

name_variants.name: O Segundo Reinado
name_variants.context: The 'Second Reign' of Pedro II, distinguished from the brief First Reign of Pedro I (1822-1831)
name_variants.source: Needell, Jeffrey D. The Party of Order: The Conservatives, the State, and Slavery in the Brazilian Monarchy (2006)

territory_changes.description: Pedro I declared independence from Portugal on September 7, 1822 ('Grito do Ipiranga'). Unlike Spanish America, Brazil's independence was proclaimed by the Portuguese royal heir himself, resulting in a relatively peaceful transition that preserved the monarchy and the institution of slavery.

War of the Triple Alliance (Paraguayan War): Brazil, Argentina, and Uruguay devastated Paraguay. Estimated 60-90% of Paraguay's male population was killed. Brazil used enslaved and freed Black soldiers. The war consolidated Brazil's control over Mato Grosso and southern borders.

Military coup on November 15, 1889 deposed Pedro II and proclaimed the Republic. The abolition of slavery in 1888 had alienated the slaveholding elite who had been the monarchy's base of support. Pedro II was exiled to Europe. The formerly enslaved population received no land, reparations, or meaningful economic integration.

sources.citation: Barman, Roderick J. Citizen Emperor: Pedro II and the Making of Brazil, 1825-1891. Stanford University Press, 1999
Schwarcz, Lilia Moritz. The Emperor's Beard: Dom Pedro II and the Tropical Monarchy of Brazil, 1831-1889. Hill and Wang, 2004
Conrad, Robert Edgar. The Destruction of Brazilian Slavery, 1850-1888. University of California Press, 1972

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-002: The 'Pirate Republic' of Nassau was a loosely organized community of pirates who occupied New Providence Island in the Bahamas. While romanticized in popular culture, pirate communities operated in the context of the Atlantic slave trade, colonial exploitation, and maritime violence. Some pirates, notably Black Sam Bellamy, were noted for relatively egalitarian practices including racial integration of crews. However, pirates also participated in the slave trade, and many had previously served as privateers or in the Royal Navy. The democratic governance aspects (voting on captains, dividing plunder) are historically documented but should not obscure the violence of piracy. The Bahamas were Taíno/Lucayan territory before European colonization wiped out the indigenous population. ETHICS-001: 'Republic of Pirates' is a modern popularized term; the pirates did not formally declare a republic.

name_variants.name: Pirate Republic of Nassau
name_variants.context: Modern popular designation; the pirates themselves did not use this term
name_variants.source: Woodard, Colin. The Republic of Pirates (2007)

name_variants.name: Flying Gang
name_variants.context: Name for the loose confederation of pirates operating out of Nassau, including Blackbeard, Charles Vane, and Benjamin Hornigold
name_variants.source: Rediker, Marcus. Villains of All Nations: Atlantic Pirates in the Golden Age (2004)

territory_changes.description: Spanish and French forces raided and destroyed the English settlement on New Providence during the War of the Spanish Succession. With colonial authority collapsed, pirates began using Nassau as a base of operations. The natural harbor provided shelter and the island's location was ideal for intercepting Caribbean shipping lanes.

Following the Treaty of Utrecht (1713), thousands of unemployed privateers turned to piracy. Nassau became the de facto pirate capital under figures like Benjamin Hornigold, Edward Teach (Blackbeard), Charles Vane, Jack Rackham, and Anne Bonny. An estimated 500-700 pirates operated from Nassau at its peak. Crews practiced a form of democratic governance.

Woodes Rogers arrived as the first Royal Governor, offering the King's Pardon to pirates who surrendered. Many accepted, including Hornigold. Those who refused (Charles Vane, Jack Rackham) were hunted down. Rogers hanged eight pirates in December 1718, ending the pirate republic.

sources.citation: Woodard, Colin. The Republic of Pirates: Being the True and Surprising Story of the Caribbean Pirates and the Man Who Brought Them Down. Harcourt, 2007
Rediker, Marcus. Villains of All Nations: Atlantic Pirates in the Golden Age. Beacon Press, 2004
A General History of the Pyrates (attributed to Captain Charles Johnson, likely Daniel Defoe), 1724 — principal primary source for Golden Age piracy

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-002: The Duchy of Courland was one of the smallest European states to participate in transatlantic colonialism. Duke Jakob Kettler's colonies in Tobago and on the Gambia River (James Island/Kunta Kinteh Island) were directly integrated into the Atlantic slave trade. Courlandian traders purchased enslaved Africans from their Gambian colony for transport to Tobago plantations. The Courlanders' colonization of Tobago displaced the indigenous Kalinago (Island Carib) people. The colony changed hands multiple times as larger colonial powers contested control. ETHICS-001: This entry documents the often-overlooked participation of Baltic European states in the slave trade and colonialism.

name_variants.name: Courland Colony of Tobago
name_variants.context: English designation for the Courlandian settlement on Tobago
name_variants.source: Merritt, J.E. The Courlanders in Tobago, Caribbean Quarterly (1959)

name_variants.name: Neukurland (New Courland)
name_variants.context: German name for the colony; the Courlanders named their settlement 'New Courland'
name_variants.source: Mattiesen, Otto Heinz. Die Kolonial- und Übersee-politik der kurländischen Herzöge (1940)

territory_changes.description: Duke Friedrich of Courland established the first Courlandian settlement on Tobago. The initial settlement failed but was re-established under Duke Jakob in 1642 as 'New Courland' with Fort Jacobus. Approximately 120 settlers and enslaved Africans were brought to the island.

During Duke Jakob's captivity by Sweden (1658-1660, Northern Wars), Dutch forces seized the Tobago colony. The colony was recaptured and lost multiple times. The instability reflected Courland's inability to protect distant colonies while being a small vassal duchy in the Baltic.

Courland permanently lost control of Tobago to larger colonial powers. The colony had been contested among Courland, the Netherlands, France, and England. Duke Jakob's death (1681) and Courland's declining power made colonial ventures unsustainable. Tobago would subsequently change hands between European powers numerous times.

sources.citation: Merritt, J.E. 'The Courlanders in Tobago.' Caribbean Quarterly 5:3, 1959
Mattiesen, Otto Heinz. Die Kolonial- und Übersee-politik der kurländischen Herzöge. Stuttgart, 1940

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Kingdom of Mosquitia was a Miskitu polity on the Caribbean coast of present-day Nicaragua and Honduras. The Miskitu had a complex relationship with British colonialism: British alliance provided weapons and diplomatic recognition that allowed the Miskitu to resist Spanish colonization and maintain autonomy, but also drew them into the slave trade (raiding neighboring indigenous groups for captives to sell to British buyers). The British used the Miskitu as proxies for their geopolitical interests. The kingdom was ultimately dissolved by Nicaraguan and Honduran military occupation in 1894 — the 'Reincorporación' — over Miskitu objections. This forced incorporation and subsequent marginalization of the Miskitu people continues to generate political tension. ETHICS-001: The spelling 'Mosquito' is a colonial anglicization/hispanicization; 'Miskitu' is closer to the people's own pronunciation.

name_variants.name: Mosquito Kingdom
name_variants.context: English colonial designation; 'Mosquito' from the Miskitu people's name, not from the insect
name_variants.source: Offen, Karl H. The Miskitu Kingdom: Landscape and the Emergence of a Miskitu Ethnic Identity (1999)

name_variants.name: Mosquito Coast / Mosquitia
name_variants.context: Geographic designation for the coastal territory; persists in modern usage
name_variants.source: Helms, Mary W. Asang: Adaptations to Culture Contact in a Miskito Community (1971)

name_variants.name: Miskitu Indian Kingdom
name_variants.context: Designation used in Miskitu historical memory; the kings bore European-style titles (George I, etc.) granted by Britain
name_variants.source: Dennis, Philip A. & Olien, Michael D. Kingship among the Miskito (1984)

territory_changes.region: Caribbean coast of Central America (present-day Nicaragua and Honduras)
territory_changes.description: Emergence of the Miskitu kingdom through alliance with English and Dutch traders and pirates. The Miskitu acquired firearms and used them to dominate neighboring indigenous groups. A kingship system was established, with the first recorded king crowned by the English in 1687. The kingdom controlled the coast from Cabo Gracias a Dios to Bluefields.

Treaty of Managua between Britain and Nicaragua. Britain withdrew formal protection of the Miskitu Kingdom but secured an autonomous 'Mosquito Reserve' within Nicaragua. The Miskitu king was demoted to 'hereditary chief.' This compromise reflected declining British interest in the region and U.S. pressure (Clayton-Bulwer Treaty of 1850).

Nicaraguan military forcibly occupied Bluefields and dissolved the Mosquito Reserve — the 'Reincorporación.' Chief Robert Henry Clarence was deposed. The British protested but did not intervene. Miskitu autonomy was extinguished. Honduras had similarly absorbed its portion of the Mosquito Coast. Miskitu land rights remain contested to this day.

sources.citation: Offen, Karl H. 'The Miskitu Kingdom: Landscape and the Emergence of a Miskitu Ethnic Identity, Northeastern Nicaragua and Honduras, 1600-1800.' PhD dissertation, University of Texas at Austin, 1999
Helms, Mary W. Asang: Adaptations to Culture Contact in a Miskito Community. University of Florida Press, 1971
Naylor, Robert A. Penny Ante Imperialism: The Mosquito Shore and the Bay of Honduras, 1600-1914. Fairleigh Dickinson University Press, 1989

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Arawak (Lokono) peoples were among the first Caribbean populations encountered and decimated by European colonizers. Spanish colonization from 1492 onward devastated Arawakan-speaking populations across the Caribbean through enslavement, massacre, and disease. Columbus enslaved hundreds of Arawak people on his first voyages. The distinction between 'Arawak' and 'Carib' peoples was exploited by Europeans: peoples classified as 'Carib' could be legally enslaved (as 'cannibals'), while 'Arawak' were nominally protected — a distinction that was manipulated arbitrarily. ETHICS-001: 'Arawak' is a broad linguistic and cultural grouping, not a single polity. The endonym 'Lokono' refers specifically to the mainland South American Arawak people. Taíno and other Caribbean groups also spoke Arawakan languages but were distinct peoples. Using 'Arawak' as a single entry is a simplification of enormous cultural diversity.

name_variants.name: Arawak
name_variants.context: General term for Arawakan-speaking peoples of the Caribbean and South American coast; a broad linguistic-cultural label, not a political entity
name_variants.source: Heckenberger, Michael J. The Ecology of Power: Culture, Place, and Personhood in the Southern Amazon (2005)

name_variants.name: Lokono
name_variants.context: Endonym for the mainland Arawak people of the Guianas and Venezuela; means 'the people'
name_variants.source: Hill, Jonathan D. & Santos-Granero, Fernando. Comparative Arawakan Histories (2002)

name_variants.name: Island Arawak / Neo-Taíno
name_variants.context: Older archaeological designation for pre-Columbian Caribbean Arawakan cultures; now largely superseded
name_variants.source: Rouse, Irving. The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus (1992)

territory_changes.region: Caribbean islands and northeastern South American coast
territory_changes.description: Arawakan-speaking peoples expanded from the Orinoco River basin into the Lesser Antilles and Greater Antilles over several centuries. They developed agricultural societies based on cassava cultivation, established extensive trade networks, and created complex ceramic traditions. This expansion represents one of the most significant migrations in pre-Columbian American history.

Columbus's arrival initiated the destruction of Arawakan Caribbean societies. Spanish colonization targeted Arawakan-speaking peoples across the Caribbean for forced labor and enslavement. The Lucayan Arawak of the Bahamas were entirely removed and enslaved within two decades of contact. Epidemic disease caused catastrophic mortality across all Arawakan populations.

By 1600, Arawakan populations in the Greater Antilles and Bahamas were functionally extinct as distinct societies due to enslavement, massacre, and disease. Mainland Lokono communities in the Guianas survived and continue to exist today. The cultural diversity of dozens of distinct Arawakan peoples was reduced to small remnant communities. Modern genetic studies show significant Arawakan ancestry in Caribbean populations.

sources.citation: Hill, Jonathan D. & Santos-Granero, Fernando (eds.). Comparative Arawakan Histories: Rethinking Language Family and Culture Area in Amazonia. University of Illinois Press, 2002
Heckenberger, Michael J. The Ecology of Power: Culture, Place, and Personhood in the Southern Amazon, AD 1000-2000. Routledge, 2005
Rouse, Irving. The Tainos: Rise and Decline of the People Who Greeted Columbus. Yale University Press, 1992

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-002: Poverty Point is one of the oldest and most complex archaeological sites in North America, predating Cahokia by nearly 2,000 years. The monumental earthworks (including a 72-foot-tall mound) demonstrate sophisticated engineering and social organization among pre-agricultural hunter-gatherer-fisher peoples — challenging outdated assumptions that complex societies required agriculture. The modern name 'Poverty Point' comes from a 19th-century plantation on the site — an ironic and colonial name for one of North America's most remarkable pre-Columbian achievements. ETHICS-001: The identity and language of the builders are entirely unknown. All knowledge comes from archaeological evidence. The site name erases the builders' achievement behind a plantation name.

name_variants.name: Poverty Point
name_variants.context: Modern name from a 19th-century cotton plantation that occupied the site; the original name is lost
name_variants.source: Gibson, Jon L. The Ancient Mounds of Poverty Point (2000)

name_variants.name: Poverty Point Culture
name_variants.context: Archaeological designation for the broader cultural complex associated with the site
name_variants.source: Webb, Clarence H. The Poverty Point Culture (1982)

territory_changes.description: Emergence of the Poverty Point complex: a network of sites centered on the main site in present-day West Carroll Parish, Louisiana. The main site features six concentric C-shaped earthen ridges and several mounds. Trade networks extended from the Great Lakes to the Gulf Coast, importing stone, copper, and galena from across eastern North America.

Peak construction period. Mound A (the Bird Mound), standing 72 feet high, was likely constructed in a single massive building episode — one of the most impressive engineering feats in pre-Columbian North America. The site served as a major trade center and possible ceremonial complex. Population estimates are debated; the site may have been a seasonal gathering place rather than a permanent city.

The Poverty Point complex declined and the main site was abandoned. Reasons are unknown — possibly environmental change, shifts in trade networks, or social transformation. Mound-building traditions in the lower Mississippi Valley would not reach comparable scale again for centuries.

sources.citation: Gibson, Jon L. The Ancient Mounds of Poverty Point: Place of Rings. University Press of Florida, 2000
Webb, Clarence H. The Poverty Point Culture. Geoscience and Man Vol. 17, Louisiana State University, 1982
Kidder, Tristram R. 'Transforming Poverty Point.' American Antiquity 83:2, 2018, pp. 196-219

ethical_notes: ETHICS-002: The Ancestral Puebloans (formerly misnamed 'Anasazi,' a Navajo word meaning 'ancient enemies' or 'ancestors of enemies') built the famous cliff dwellings of Mesa Verde and other sites across the Four Corners region. Many modern Pueblo peoples consider these sites sacred ancestral places. The term 'Anasazi' is considered offensive by many Pueblo people and its continued use in some archaeological literature is contested. The reasons for the 13th-century depopulation of Mesa Verde are debated; drought, resource depletion, social conflict, and violence all played roles. Archaeological evidence of violence in the final period is significant but should not be used to perpetuate stereotypes. ETHICS-001: 'Ancestral Puebloan' is the preferred archaeological term replacing 'Anasazi.' Modern Hopi, Zuni, Acoma, and other Pueblo peoples are direct descendants. Their oral traditions about these sites should be considered alongside archaeological evidence.

name_variants.name: Ancestral Puebloans
name_variants.context: Current preferred academic term replacing 'Anasazi'; emphasizes continuity with modern Pueblo peoples
name_variants.source: Lekson, Stephen H. A History of the Ancient Southwest (2009)

name_variants.name: Anasazi
name_variants.context: Navajo word meaning 'ancient enemies' or 'ancestors of enemies'; considered offensive by many Pueblo people. Still used in older literature
name_variants.source: Lipe, William D. 'Notes from the North.' In The Archaeology of Chaco Canyon, ed. Lekson (2006)

name_variants.name: Hisatsinom
name_variants.context: Hopi name meaning 'the ancient people'; used by Hopi to describe their ancestors who built Mesa Verde and other sites
name_variants.source: Ferguson, T.J. & Colwell-Chanthaphonh, Chip. History Is in the Land: Multivocal Tribal Traditions in Arizona (2006)

territory_changes.region: Four Corners region (present-day CO, UT, AZ, NM)
territory_changes.description: Pueblo I period: transition from pithouse villages to above-ground masonry pueblos. Population growth across the Four Corners region. Development of distinctive black-on-white pottery traditions. Mesa Verde area emerged as a major population center with hundreds of small villages.

territory_changes.region: Mesa Verde and surrounding canyon country
territory_changes.description: Construction of the famous cliff dwellings began at Mesa Verde, including Cliff Palace (150+ rooms), Spruce Tree House, and Balcony House. These represent the peak of Ancestral Puebloan architectural achievement at Mesa Verde. The move into cliff alcoves may reflect defensive concerns as inter-community conflict increased during the late 1100s.

Complete depopulation of the Mesa Verde region by ca. 1280-1300 CE. A severe drought (the Great Drought, 1276-1299) coincided with social upheaval and evidence of violence. The population migrated south and east to the Rio Grande valley, Zuni, Hopi, and Acoma. This was not an abandonment but a migration — the people and their cultural traditions continued in new locations.

sources.citation: Lekson, Stephen H. A History of the Ancient Southwest. School for Advanced Research Press, 2009
Noble, David Grant (ed.). The Mesa Verde World: Explorations in Ancestral Puebloan Archaeology. School of American Research Press, 2006
Kohler, Timothy A. et al. 'Mesa Verde Migrations.' American Scientist 96:2, 2008

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-002: The 'Kingdom of Quito' is one of the most contested entities in South American historiography. The traditional narrative, originating with the colonial-era Jesuit historian Juan de Velasco (1789), describes a pre-Inca kingdom of the Quitu-Cara people. Modern archaeology has found no evidence supporting Velasco's account of a unified Quitu kingdom. The narrative likely served Ecuadorian nationalist purposes — providing a pre-Inca legitimating origin story. However, dismissing the concept entirely may erase genuine pre-Inca political complexity in the region. Archaeological evidence does show complex chiefdoms (señoríos) in the northern Andes before Inca conquest, including the Caranqui, Cayambe, and Panzaleo peoples. ETHICS-001: The low confidence score reflects genuine academic dispute. Including this entity documents the historiographic controversy itself. Velasco's account should not be treated as reliable history, but the pre-Inca political complexity it gestures toward was real.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Quito
name_variants.context: English translation of the traditional (and disputed) narrative of a pre-Inca kingdom
name_variants.source: Velasco, Juan de. Historia del Reino de Quito en la América Meridional (1789)

name_variants.name: Reino de Quito
name_variants.context: Spanish designation from colonial-era histories; the primary source is Juan de Velasco's 1789 account

name_variants.source: Salomon, Frank. Native Lords of Quito in the Age of the Incas (1986)

territory_changes.description: Traditional narrative: emergence of the Quitu-Cara polity through merger of coastal Cara people and highland Quitu. Modern archaeology: the northern Ecuadorian highlands hosted multiple complex chiefdoms (señoríos) including the Caranqui, Cayambe, Otavalo, Panzaleo, and Puruhá. These were independent polities, not a unified kingdom. Archaeological evidence shows monumental mound construction, intensive agriculture, and long-distance trade.

Inca expansion under Tupac Inca Yupanqui and later Huayna Capac conquered the northern Andean chiefdoms. The Caranqui and Cayambe peoples resisted fiercely; the Battle of Yahuarcocha ('Blood Lake,' ca. 1487) reportedly ended with thousands of Cayambe-Caranqui warriors massacred on the shores of the lake. Huayna Capac established Quito as a secondary Inca capital and married local nobility.

sources.citation: Salomon, Frank. Native Lords of Quito in the Age of the Incas: The Political Economy of North Andean Chiefdoms. Cambridge University Press, 1986
Velasco, Juan de. Historia del Reino de Quito en la América Meridional, 1789 — primary source for the traditional narrative, now considered largely mythological
Athens, J. Stephen. 'Ethnicity and Adaptation: The Late Period-Cara Occupation in Northern Highland Ecuador.' PhD dissertation, University of New Mexico, 1992

ethical_notes:
ETHICS-002: The Wanka (Huanca) were a major confederation of the central Peruvian highlands who were conquered by the Inca Empire under Pachacuti around 1460. The Wanka strongly resisted Inca domination, and when Spanish conquistadors arrived in 1533, the Wanka allied with the Spanish against the Inca — a strategic choice born of Inca oppression that has led to complex historical judgment. The Spanish subsequently exploited Wanka labor through the encomienda and mita systems despite the alliance. The Wanka's role illustrates how colonial conquest exploited inter-indigenous rivalries. ETHICS-001: The complexity of indigenous agency in the colonial period must be represented: the Wanka alliance with Spain against the Inca was a rational response to Inca domination, not collaboration in the colonial sense. Both Inca conquest and Spanish colonization brought suffering to the Wanka people.

name_variants.context: Quechua name for the confederation; the original Wanka Quechua dialect form

name_variants.name: Huanca
name_variants.context: Hispanicized spelling; used in colonial and modern Spanish-language sources

name_variants.name: Wanka Confederation / Hatun Xauxa
name_variants.context: Refers to the three saya (divisions) of the Wanka polity: Hatun Xauxa, Lurin Wanka, and Hanan Wanka. Hatun Xauxa was the largest
name_variants.source: D'Altroy, Terence N. Provincial Power in the Inka Empire (1992)

territory_changes.description: Wanka peoples consolidated control over the Mantaro Valley, one of the most productive agricultural zones in the Andes. They developed hilltop fortified settlements (including Tunanmarca with an estimated 4,000-8,000 residents), intensive pastoralism, and long-distance trade. The confederation was organized into three sayas (divisions), each with its own leadership.

Inca conquest under Pachacuti or Tupac Inca Yupanqui. The Wanka resisted fiercely but were defeated. The Inca imposed the mita labor system, relocated populations (mitma), and established the major administrative center of Hatun Xauxa (Jauja). Wanka elites were incorporated into the Inca system but Wanka identity and resentment of Inca rule persisted.

The Wanka allied with Francisco Pizarro and the Spanish against the Inca, providing thousands of warriors and logistical support. Wanka leader Guacrapáucar provided critical military assistance at the sieges of Lima and Cusco. Despite this alliance, the Spanish subsequently imposed the encomienda system on the Wanka. The promised rewards for alliance were never fully honored.

sources.citation: D'Altroy, Terence N. & Hastorf, Christine A. Empire and Domestic Economy. Kluwer Academic/Plenum, 2001
D'Altroy, Terence N. Provincial Power in the Inka Empire. Smithsonian Institution Press, 1992
Espinoza Soriano, Waldemar. 'Los Huancas, aliados de la conquista.' Anales Científicos de la Universidad del Centro del Perú 1, 1973

ethical_notes: ETHICS-001: 'Taino' as a unified ethnic label is debated; it derives from the indigenous word meaning 'good/noble' used during first contact with Columbus. The Taino comprised multiple cacicazgos (chiefdoms) across the Greater Antilles and Bahamas. ETHICS-002: The Taino experienced one of the most catastrophic population collapses in human history, from an estimated 250,000-1,000,000+ to near-extinction within 50 years of contact, due to enslavement, disease, and violence under the encomienda system. Columbus's own journals document forced labor and mutilation. Recent genetic studies (Schroeder et al. 2018) show significant Taino ancestry persists in Caribbean populations, contradicting the 'extinction' narrative.
